messages.content:
can you explain these results in much simpler terms please:


A 2 × 2 × 3 mixed ANOVA was conducted with one between subjects factor: group (ADHD vs. TDC) and two within subject factors: noise condition (no noise vs. noise) and task (spanboard; word recall; 2-back). The ANOVA showed a positive main effect of noise [F(1,38) = 5.71, p = 0.022, η2 = 0.131]. The interaction between task and noise, i.e., noise affected tasks differently, did not reach significance but indicated a trend [F(2,76) = 3.86, p = 0.057]; also a trend toward interaction between group and noise was found [F(1,38) = 3.10, p = 0.086] indicating a larger
noise benefit for ADHD children. The between group factor for overall task performance did not reach significance, however, the TDC group performed tasks marginally superior to the ADHD group [F(1,38) = 3.71, p = 0.062].
A second 2 × 2 × 3 ANOVA was conducted, just comprising the ADHD group with three within subjects factors: noise (no noise vs. noise), medication (off vs. on medication), and task (spanboard, word recall, 2-back). This ANOVA showed a strong positive main effect of noise [F(1,19) = 16.64, p = 0.001, η2 = 0.467] and no main effect of medication [F(1,19) = 0.63, p = 0.436]. Further, an interaction between medication and noise, i.e., the benefit of noise was larger than the one of medication [F(1) = 4.88, p = 0.040, η2 = 0.204], and also an interaction between task and noise was found, noise affected the three tasks differently [F(2,18) = 11.40, p = 0.003, η2 = 0.375]. The three- way interaction between task, noise, and medication did not reach significance [F(2) = 3.13, p = 0.093].
Post hoc analyses, by using independent samples t-tests task by task, showed noise benefit in the spanboard- and word recall task but not in the 2-back task. In the spanboard task a paired samples t-test showed that performance in terms of successfully recalled dots increased significantly by introduction of noise as compared to no noise in non-medicated ADHD children [t = (19) = 3.18, p = 0.005], as well as in medicated children [t = (19) = 2.28, p = 0.034]. A (2 × 2) within subject ANOVA showed a tendency to interaction between noise and medication [F(1,19) = 3.51, p = 0.077] in this task (see Figure 1).
The control group was not influenced by noise, an independent samples t-test displayed that the TDC superior performance in the spanboard task compared to the ADHD group in the silent condition [t(38) = 2.52, p = 0.016] disappeared during noise exposure [t(38) = 0.68, p = 0.504]. The medicated children did not fully reach the performance of TDC in no noise condition [t(38) = 1.85, p = 0.072]. There was no significant effect of medication on children with ADHD in no noise condition, the improvement by medication did not reach significance [t(19) = 1.91, p = 0.071]. However, the control group did not differ significantly from the ADHD group in the presence of noise irrespective of medication or not (see Figure 1).
In the word recall task (free recall), a paired samples test showed that noise significantly improved the number of correctly recalled words compared to no noise condition in absence of medication [t = (19) = 4.92, p < 0.0005]. In the no noise condition, the non-medicated ADHD group performed worse than the TD group [t(38) = 2.45, p = 0.019]; however, this difference was eliminated by noise. No effect of medication was found in this task and no effect of noise was found in the ADHD group during medication, or in the control group. Finally we found a significant interaction between noise and medication in this task within the ADHD group [F(1,19) = 5.43, p = 0.031] (see Figure 2). Table 2 reports performance in raw score in the spanbord- and the word recall tasks for both ADHD- and TDC groups with t-statistics.
In the verbal 2-back task no significant results of either noise or medication was found in either group (see Table 3). When looking at the number of correctly recalled items both groups performed equally well [ADHD = 22,5 vs. TDC = 22,9; F(1,38) = 0.07, p = 0.787]. No interaction between group and noise and no main effect of noise on ADHD was found (p = 0.136). No effect of medication was found. There was a nominal positive effect of noise on the ADHD group but it was far from reaching any significance. However, when looking at the response time we got a significant group difference, as TDC were faster than the ADHD group [34.1 vs. 40.8 s; t(38) = 2.81,
p = 0.008]. The only effect of noise exposure was on the TDC group where noise increased speed on task performance [35.3 vs. 32.8; t(19) = 2.17, p = 0.043]. Noise had little effect on the speed of the ADHD group and the interaction between noise and group indicated a trend [F(1,38) = 3.35, p = 0.075]. At last, the effect of test occasion was non-significant, but nominally there was a slight improvement for the TDC group in the second test session.

The results of the study showed that noise had a positive effect on task performance in children with ADHD. Specifically, introducing noise improved performance in the spanboard task, where children had to recall the location of dots, as well as in the word recall task, where children had to remember and recall words. The control group, on the other hand, was not influenced by noise and performed consistently well in both the spanboard and word recall tasks regardless of the presence of noise. 

In terms of medication, there was no significant effect observed in the ADHD group. Medication did not improve performance in the no noise condition and did not interact with noise to affect task performance. 

In the verbal 2-back task, neither noise nor medication had a significant effect on performance in either group. Both the ADHD and control groups performed equally well in terms of the number of correctly recalled items. However, the control group was faster in completing the task compared to the ADHD group. 

Overall, the results suggest that noise can have a positive impact on task performance in children with ADHD, particularly in tasks that involve recalling information. However, medication did not show a significant effect, and the verbal 2-back task was not affected by either noise or medication.

The study looked at the effects of noise and medication on task performance in children with ADHD compared to a control group. They conducted two separate analyses, one with both groups and one only with the ADHD group.

In the analysis with both groups, they found that noise had a positive effect on task performance in the ADHD group. Specifically, noise improved performance in tasks where the children had to recall the location of dots and remember and recall words. However, the control group was not affected by noise and performed well in both tasks regardless of noise.

There was no significant effect of medication in the ADHD group. Medication did not improve performance in tasks with or without noise.

In the verbal 2-back task, neither noise nor medication had a significant effect on performance in either group. Both groups performed equally well in terms of the number of correctly recalled items. However, the control group completed the task faster than the ADHD group.

Overall, the results suggest that noise can benefit task performance in children with ADHD but medication did not show a significant effect. The verbal 2-back task was not affected by noise or medication.

put this in simpler words
A 2 × 2 × 3 mixed ANOVA was conducted with one between subjects factor: group (ADHD vs. TDC) and two within subject factors: noise condition (no noise vs. noise) and task (spanboard; word recall; 2-back). The ANOVA showed a positive main effect of noise [F(1,38) = 5.71, p = 0.022, η2 = 0.131]. The interaction between task and noise, i.e., noise affected tasks differently, did not reach significance but indicated a trend [F(2,76) = 3.86, p = 0.057]; also a trend toward interaction between group and noise was found [F(1,38) = 3.10, p = 0.086] indicating a larger noise benefit for ADHD children. The between group factor for overall task performance did not reach significance, however, the TDC group performed tasks marginally superior to the ADHD group [F(1,38) = 3.71, p = 0.062].

The study compared children with ADHD to a control group (TDC) and looked at how noise and different tasks affected their performance. They found that noise had a positive effect overall, meaning it helped improve performance in both groups. However, the effect was not very strong and was only a trend. They also noticed that noise affected the tasks differently for the two groups, with children with ADHD benefiting more from noise.

In terms of overall task performance, the control group did slightly better than the ADHD group, but the difference was not significant.

explain simply 
Post hoc analyses, by using independent samples t-tests task by task, showed noise benefit in the spanboard- and word recall task but not in the 2-back task. In the spanboard task a paired samples t-test showed that performance in terms of successfully recalled dots increased significantly by introduction of noise as compared to no noise in non-medicated ADHD children [t = (19) = 3.18, p = 0.005], as well as in medicated children [t = (19) = 2.28, p = 0.034]. A (2 × 2) within subject ANOVA showed a tendency to interaction between noise and medication [F(1,19) = 3.51, p = 0.077] in this task (see Figure 1).

The researchers conducted additional analyses to specifically look at the effects of noise on different tasks. They found that noise had a positive effect on performance in the spanboard and word recall tasks, meaning it helped the children do better in those tasks. However, they did not find the same positive effect of noise in the 2-back task.

In the spanboard task, they compared performance with and without noise for children with ADHD who were not taking medication and for those who were taking medication. They found that introducing noise significantly improved performance in both groups, meaning the children were able to recall the location of dots better when there was noise compared to when there wasn't.

They also used another statistical analysis to explore the interaction between noise and medication in the spanboard task. While there was not a significant interaction, there was a suggestion that the effects of noise and medication together might have an effect on performance.

Figure 1 likely shows the results and data related to these findings.

simplify: however, this difference was eliminated by noise. No effect of medication was found in this task and no effect of noise was found in the ADHD group during medication, or in the control group

However, the difference in performance between the ADHD group and the control group in the spanboard task disappeared when noise was introduced. This means that noise leveled the playing field and made their performance more similar. Medication did not have an effect on performance in this task for either group, and noise did not have an effect on the ADHD group when they were taking medication, or on the control group.

simplify 
In the verbal 2-back task no significant results of either noise or medication was found in either group (see Table 3). When looking at the number of correctly recalled items both groups performed equally well [ADHD = 22,5 vs. TDC = 22,9; F(1,38) = 0.07, p = 0.787]. No interaction between group and noise and no main effect of noise on ADHD was found (p = 0.136). No effect of medication was found. There was a nominal positive effect of noise on the ADHD group but it was far from reaching any significance. However, when looking at the response time we got a significant group difference, as TDC were faster than the ADHD group [34.1 vs. 40.8 s; t(38) = 2.81,
p = 0.008]. The only effect of noise exposure was on the TDC group where noise increased speed on task performance [35.3 vs. 32.8; t(19) = 2.17, p = 0.043]. Noise had little effect on the speed of the ADHD group and the interaction between noise and group indicated a trend [F(1,38) = 3.35, p = 0.075]. At last, the effect of test occasion was non-significant, but nominally there was a slight improvement for the TDC group in the second test session.

In the verbal 2-back task, neither noise nor medication had any significant effects in either group. Both groups performed equally well in terms of the number of correctly recalled items. There was no interaction between group and noise, and noise did not have a notable effect on the ADHD group. However, there was a significant difference in response time between the groups, as the control group was faster than the ADHD group. The introduction of noise increased the speed of task performance for the control group, but had little effect on the ADHD group. There was a trend suggesting a possible interaction between noise and group, but it was not statistically significant. There was also no significant effect of the test occasion, although there may have been a slight improvement for the control group in the second test session.

explain more simply 
However, there are few studies in which arousal is experimentally manipulated in ADHD, while arousal can be defined in different ways: quantitative EEG, alpha, beta, gamma-waves (Buyck and Wiersema, 2015), event rate (Sonuga-Barke et al., 1992; Kooistra et al., 2010), or direct physical measures as heart rate and blood pressure (Börger and van der Meere, 2000). However, when assessing energetic levels, indirect measures like event rate and work load are used as indicators where slow rates or low load are claimed to produce under-arousal, and fast rates and high load produce over-arousal (Sergeant, 2005). The problematic part is that behavioral performance is taken as a proxy for arousal state where poor performance is regarded as an indicator of state regulation deficits without providing any physiological measures as supporting evidence

There haven't been many studies that specifically look at how alertness or arousal is influenced in children with ADHD. Researchers have used different methods to measure arousal, such as brain activity patterns or physical measures like heart rate and blood pressure. However, when assessing arousal, they often rely on indirect measures like event rate or workload. Slower rates or low workloads are thought to indicate under-arousal, while faster rates or higher workloads suggest over-arousal. The problem is that researchers usually use behavioral performance as a way to infer arousal levels without directly measuring physiological indicators.

There haven't been many studies looking at how alertness is affected in children with ADHD. Researchers have used different ways to measure alertness, like brain activity or physical measures like heart rate. However, they often rely on indirect measures, like how fast or slow someone does a task, to estimate alertness instead of directly measuring it.

explain simple :
Another strong candidate for the cause of noise benefit is auditory masking, where a masker different from the signal can facilitate signal detection (Durlach et al., 2003). In ADHD It has been shown that if the masker is predictable children behave less impulsively (Gray et al., 2002). Moreover, masking effects have been shown in both visual and tactile modalities (Tan et al., 2003; Dawes et al., 2009). One possible way to determine the role of masking in noise benefit is to use stochastic vestibular noise (SVS) that seems to have the same or a similar effect on performance as auditory noise

One possible reason for the benefit of noise is something called auditory masking. This is when a background noise can actually help in detecting a signal. In ADHD, studies have shown that if the background noise is predictable, children are less impulsive. Masking effects have also been observed in other senses like vision and touch. To further understand the role of masking in the benefit of noise, researchers can use a type of noise called stochastic vestibular noise (SVS) that has a similar effect on performance as auditory noise.
